stem: A factory produces pharmaceutical tablets using two sources. Batch Process Alpha handles 60% of production with a known defect rate of 1.9%. Batch Process Beta handles 40% of production, but its defect rate is uncertain — recent quality audits suggest it lies between 6.4% and 10.2%, depending on raw material batch quality. A randomly selected pharmaceutical tablet is found to be defective. What is the probability it came from Batch Process Beta?

ground_truth.answer: P(Batch Process Beta | defective) ranges from 0.692 to 0.782 as Batch Process Beta's defect rate varies from 6.4% to 10.2%. Point estimate at 8.3%: 0.744.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(B|def) = P(def|B)×P(B) / [P(def|A)×P(A) + P(def|B)×P(B)].
The interval [0.692, 0.782] captures the range of posterior probabilities.
common_failure_modes: Using only the midpoint defect rate and providing a single answer
Comparing defect rates directly without applying Bayes' theorem
